Content Prep Guide

Scalp Health Lab
Teaching Reference

Deep content, science, and talking points behind every segment of the PlayLab Beauty workshop. Refresh before the class or reference on a tablet during.
PlayLab Beauty - Sunday, March 1, 2026 - 60 Minutes - 8-12 Guests
1

Welcome & Room Read

10:00 - 10:08 (8 min) - Led by Tina

Purpose

Set the tone: warm, educational, not salesy. Establish yourself as someone who has been doing this for 40+ years and genuinely wants to help. The name tent exercise does two things: it shows guests you care about their individual concerns, and it gives you data to reference throughout the class.

Opening line: "Welcome to the Scalp Health Lab. I'm Tina, I've been a trichologist and stylist for over 40 years, and this is my daughter Jade. Today we're going to learn about your scalp, practice gua sha together, and each of you will get a one-on-one scalp scan from Jade."
Name tent prompt: "Take a moment to write your number one scalp concern on your name card. It could be anything - dryness, thinning, oiliness, itching - whatever comes to mind first. We'll address as many as we can."

Reading the Room

Quickly scan the name tents. Most common concerns you'll see:

  • Thinning/hair loss - This is the big one. Acknowledge it early: "Several of you wrote about thinning - you're not alone, and there's a lot we can do."
  • Dry/itchy scalp - Often related to barrier dysfunction. Connect to pH and microbiome during Segment 2.
  • Oiliness - Usually a compensatory response to over-stripping. Explain during the Reset step.
  • Dandruff/flaking - Malassezia overgrowth + inflammation. Reference during 4R Protocol.

Use these concerns as callbacks throughout: "Remember, [Name] asked about thinning - this is exactly why micro-circulation matters."

2

Scalp Science

10:08 - 10:18 (10 min) - Led by Tina

Your Scalp Is Skin

The single most important reframe in this class. Most people think of "hair care" and "skin care" as separate things. Your scalp is skin - the same organ as your face, just with more hair follicles and sebaceous glands.

Key phrase: "Your scalp is the most overlooked skin on your body. You moisturize your face every day. When was the last time you thought about the skin under your hair?"
Analogy: "Think of your scalp like a garden. The hair is what grows above the surface, but everything depends on the soil underneath. If the soil is dry, depleted, or compacted, nothing thrives."
Teaching visual (draw on whiteboard or gesture): Describe a cross-section of the scalp from top to bottom: "Picture a slice through your scalp. On top, the epidermis - that's the outer surface you can touch. Below that, the dermis, full of blood vessels and nerve endings. Then the subcutaneous layer with fat tissue. And sitting in all of that, like plants in soil, are the hair follicles - each one with its own blood supply, its own sebaceous gland producing oil, and its own tiny muscle."
Deep Reference

Scalp anatomy - 5 layers: Skin, Connective subcutaneous tissue, Galea Aponeurotica (aponeurosis + muscles), Loose areolar tissue, Pericranium. The acronym SCALP.

Sebaceous gland density: The scalp has 400-900 sebaceous glands per cm² - the highest density anywhere on the human body. These produce sebum, the natural oil that protects the scalp barrier.

Hair follicle density: 124-200 hairs per cm², approximately 100,000 follicles on the average scalp.

Scalp pH: Healthy scalp sits between 4.5-5.5 (slightly acidic). This acid mantle supports beneficial microbes and acts as a barrier against pathogens.

Ludovici M, et al. Influence of sebaceous gland density on the stratum corneum lipidome. Sci Rep. 2018;8:11500 [VERIFIED]
Jimenez F, et al. Distribution of human hair in follicular units. Dermatol Surg. 1999;25(4):294-8 [VERIFIED]
Phillips CM, Weston K. Anatomy, Head and Neck, Scalp. StatPearls. 2023 [VERIFIED]

Micro-circulation

This is the "why" behind everything else in the class. Hair follicles need blood flow to deliver nutrients and oxygen. When circulation is restricted - from tension, inflammation, or environmental damage - follicles don't get what they need, and growth slows or stops.

Key phrase: "Every hair follicle needs its own blood supply. When blood flow slows down, the follicle gets less oxygen, fewer nutrients, and growth starts to stall."
Healthy vs. restricted - use your hands to demonstrate: "Imagine two hair follicles. One has healthy blood flow - vessels are open, nutrients are reaching the follicle, everything it needs is getting delivered. The other has restricted flow - the vessels are narrowed from chronic tension, stress, or inflammation. Same follicle, completely different environment. That's the difference between hair that thrives and hair that stalls."
Deep Reference

VEGF and hair growth: VEGF (Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor) controls blood vessel formation around follicles. Research showed a fourfold increase in perifollicular vessel size during the active growth phase (anagen). When researchers boosted VEGF in mice, follicles grew 30% larger. When they blocked VEGF, follicles shrank by 30%.

Why this matters for the class: You don't need to say "VEGF." The takeaway is: more blood flow = bigger, healthier follicles. Gua sha and scalp massage mechanically increase this blood flow.

Scalp massage data: Standardized scalp massage (4 min/day for 24 weeks) resulted in measurably increased hair thickness. The mechanical stretching was shown to increase expression of hair growth genes and decrease expression of hair loss genes at the cellular level.

Yano K, Brown LF, Detmar M. Control of hair growth and follicle size by VEGF-mediated angiogenesis. J Clin Invest. 2001;107(4):409-417 [VERIFIED]
Koyama T, et al. Standardized scalp massage results in increased hair thickness. Eplasty. 2016;16:e8 [VERIFIED]
English RS Jr, et al. Self-assessments of standardized scalp massages for androgenic alopecia. Dermatol Ther (Heidelb). 2019;9(1):167-178 [VERIFIED]

The Connected Body - Fascia

This is the concept that surprises people most. Your jaw, temples, and scalp are physically connected by a continuous layer of fascia (connective tissue). When you clench your jaw - from stress, from tension, from a long day - that tension travels up through the temples into the scalp.

Key phrase: "Here's something most people don't realize about tension. Put your hands on the sides of your head, just above your ears. Now clench your jaw. Feel that? The tension in your jaw is connected all the way up to the top of your head through a layer of connective tissue called fascia."
Interactive moment: Have everyone do the jaw clench with their hands on their temples. The physical sensation makes the concept click immediately. "That tension you just felt? Imagine that happening all day, every day, for months. That's chronic scalp tension."
The chain (trace it with your hand): "The tension chain runs like this: your jaw muscles connect into the fascia at your temples. That fascia continues up over the top of your head as a thick sheet called the galea. And the galea connects all the way down the back to your occipital muscles at the nape. Jaw, temples, crown, nape - one continuous system. When we do gua sha, we're working along this entire chain."
Deep Reference

Galea aponeurotica: The tough fibrous sheet covering the top of the skull. It connects the frontalis muscle (forehead) to the occipitalis muscle (back of head), with the temporalis muscle on each side. When these muscles tense chronically, the galea transmits that force to the scalp tissue above it.

Scalp tension theory: Research using finite element analysis found a highly significant correlation (r = -0.885, P < 0.001) between elastic scalp deformation and male pattern baldness progression. In balding areas, scalp skin is tightly bound to the galea, while in non-balding regions it slides freely. The proposed mechanism: stretch-induced mechanotransduction in dermal papilla cells triggered by involuntary muscle contraction.

Fascial continuity: The temporoparietal fascia is continuous with the SMAS (superficial musculoaponeurotic system) below the zygomatic arch, creating a unified fascial layer from the scalp down to the clavicle. This is why jaw tension, neck tension, and scalp tension are physically linked.

Teaching note: You don't need to name the specific fascial layers. The key point: jaw, temple, and scalp are one connected system. Releasing tension in one area helps the others.

Tellez-Segura R. Involvement of mechanical stress in androgenetic alopecia. Int J Trichol. 2015;7(3):95-99 [VERIFIED]
Reece EM, et al. The temporoparietal fascia. Plast Reconstr Surg. 2009;123(4):1373-81 [VERIFIED]
Moss CJ, et al. The fascial planes of the temple and face. Br J Plast Surg. 2003;56(7):623-33 [VERIFIED]

Why Gua Sha Works

Bridge the science to the practical. Gua sha has been practiced for thousands of years in traditional Chinese medicine. What's exciting is that modern research is now showing why it works at the physiological level.

Key phrase: "Gua sha isn't a trend - it's a practice with thousands of years of history. And modern science is finally catching up to explain what practitioners have known all along."
Three benefits to mention:
1. "It increases blood flow to the surface - research shows a fourfold increase in micro-circulation at the treated area."
2. "It triggers an antioxidant response in your body - your cells actually produce protective enzymes."
3. "It releases the fascia tension we just talked about - moving the tissue, not just the hair."
Deep Reference

Nielsen study (the landmark paper): A pilot study in healthy subjects using laser Doppler imaging found that gua sha caused a fourfold increase in micro-circulation at the treated area for the first 7.5 minutes, with statistically significant elevation persisting for 25 minutes (P < 0.001). The treated area showed sustained hyperemia well beyond the treatment itself.

HO-1 upregulation: Research using bioluminescence imaging showed gua sha triggers systemic upregulation of heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1) gene expression across multiple organs, persisting for over 120 hours. HO-1 is a cytoprotective enzyme that defends against oxidative stress. This means gua sha doesn't just increase blood flow locally - it triggers a body-wide protective response.

Mechanism of action: The controlled pressure of gua sha causes microscopic blood vessel expansion and extravasation (release of blood components into surrounding tissue). This triggers an immune response that includes increased ratios of immune-active cells and the production of anti-inflammatory mediators.

Important nuance: Most gua sha research has been conducted on back and body tissue, not specifically on scalp. However, the physiological mechanisms (microcirculation increase, HO-1 response, fascial release) are applicable to scalp tissue. No peer-reviewed studies have specifically measured gua sha effects on hair follicle growth.

Nielsen A, et al. The effect of Gua Sha treatment on the microcirculation of surface tissue. Explore (NY). 2007;3(5):456-66 [VERIFIED]
Kwong KK, et al. Bioluminescence imaging of heme oxygenase-1 upregulation in the Gua Sha procedure. J Vis Exp. 2009;(30):1385 [VERIFIED]
Chan ST, et al. Guasha-induced hepatoprotection in chronic active hepatitis B. Clinics (Sao Paulo). 2011;66(9):1563-1566 [VERIFIED]
3

Gua Sha Demo & Practice

10:18 - 10:35 (17 min) - Tina demos, Jade scans

The Tool - Sandalwood Gua Sha

Before demonstrating technique, introduce the sandalwood gua sha itself. Why sandalwood? It's a deliberate choice:

  • Naturally smooth grain - glides without catching on hair
  • Antimicrobial properties - sandalwood is naturally resistant to bacteria, so it stays cleaner between uses
  • Warm to the touch - unlike jade or metal tools, sandalwood doesn't shock the skin with cold, which is especially important on a sensitive scalp
  • Traditional material - used in gua sha practice for centuries
Key phrase: "We chose 100% sandalwood for a reason. Feel how smooth it is, how warm. Most gua sha tools are designed for the face - jade, metal, crystal. This one is designed specifically for the scalp. It won't catch your hair, it won't shock your skin, and sandalwood is naturally antimicrobial."

The 4 Steps

Walk through each step slowly. Demonstrate on yourself first, then have everyone practice before moving to the next step. The key technique principle: move the skin, not just the hair. Light to medium pressure. The broad flat surface should make contact with the scalp.

Before starting: "The most important thing to remember: we're moving the scalp tissue, not scratching the surface. Light to medium pressure. If it feels like you're scraping, ease up. This should feel good - like a scalp massage with more structure."
Step 1

Hairline

Start at the center of the forehead hairline. Broad, slow strokes moving from front to back toward the crown. 8-10 strokes. This releases the frontalis muscle and opens circulation from the face into the scalp.

GV24 (Shenting) - "Spirit Court"
Step 2

Crown

Move to the crown of the head. Circular motions, or radial strokes outward from the center point. 8-10 strokes per direction. The crown is where the galea aponeurotica is thickest and tension accumulates most.

GV20 (Baihui) - "Hundred Meetings"
Step 3

Temples

Broad strokes from the temples upward toward the crown. Also small circles directly on the temple. 8-10 strokes each side. This is where most people hold tension - the temporalis muscle and its fascial connection to the jaw.

GB8 (Shuaigu) - "Leading Valley"
Step 4

Nape

Strokes from the nape of the neck upward toward the crown, and also outward along the base of the skull. 8-10 strokes. The occipital region connects to the neck and shoulders, where tension commonly originates.

GB20 (Fengchi) - "Wind Pool"

Meridian Points - Teaching Reference

You don't need to teach detailed TCM theory, but having awareness of the key meridian points adds credibility and depth. If someone asks "why these specific spots?", you have the answer.

GV20 Baihui
GV24 Shenting
GB8 Shuaigu
GB20 Fengchi
Deep Reference

GV20 (Baihui) - "Hundred Meetings": Located at the crown, at the intersection of the line connecting the ear apexes and the midline. In TCM, this is the meeting point of all yang energy. Research confirms GV20-based acupuncture shows significant effects on cerebral blood flow. This is the most important point in scalp gua sha.

GB20 (Fengchi) - "Wind Pool": Located at the base of the skull, in the hollow between the two large neck muscles. Used in TCM for headache, neck stiffness, and clearing the head. Anatomically, this sits directly over where the occipital artery enters the scalp.

GV24 (Shenting) - "Spirit Court": Located at the hairline, directly above the nose. Used for calming the mind and clearing the forehead. The hairline is where the supraorbital and supratrochlear arteries supply the frontal scalp.

GB8 (Shuaigu) - "Leading Valley": Located above the ear, in the temple region. Used for temporal headaches. Sits over the superficial temporal artery, the primary blood supply to the lateral scalp.

Modern research connection: Studies using electrical impedance measurement have confirmed that activity along meridian lines is greater than surrounding tissue, with measurable changes in blood flow, temperature, and metabolic activity along these pathways.

Liu AJ, et al. Systematic review and meta-analysis of Baihui (GV20)-based scalp acupuncture. Sci Rep. 2014;4:3981 [VERIFIED]
Ahn AC, et al. Electrical impedance along connective tissue planes associated with acupuncture meridians. BMC Complement Altern Med. 2005;5:10 [VERIFIED]
WHO Standard Acupuncture Point Locations. 2014 [VERIFIED]
If someone asks about meridians: "In traditional Chinese medicine, these points are where energy pathways meet. What we know from modern research is that these same locations correspond to where major blood vessels supply the scalp. So whether you think of it as energy flow or blood flow, these are the spots that matter."
4

The 4R Protocol

10:35 - 10:45 (10 min) - Both

Framework Overview

The 4R system is how everything connects. Gua sha is the Reactivate step, but it works best as part of the complete protocol. The order matters: you reset the environment, repair the damage, reinforce the barrier, then reactivate circulation.

Key phrase: "Now that you know the technique, let's talk about the complete system. The gua sha you just practiced is one piece of a four-step protocol we call the 4Rs: Reset, Repair, Reinforce, Reactivate."
Philosophy line: "Hair growth depends on the environment around the follicle - like soil for a plant. When the scalp is imbalanced, follicles can't thrive. The 4R protocol resets the follicular microenvironment, step by step."

The 4Rs - Deep Content

Step 1: Reset

Purpose: Purify the scalp of buildup that suffocates follicles and irritates the barrier.

How: Use a purifying shampoo. Focus on scalp only (not the lengths). Add water and emulsify longer than you think. Double cleanse: first wash lifts, second wash treats.

Common mistakes to mention: Skip harsh scrubs and plastic scalp brushes. Don't shampoo with your nails. Avoid hot water (worsens sensitivity). Don't try to "train your hair" by skipping washes, especially after heavy exposure days.

Deep Reference - Reset

Why sulfate-free matters: Many conventional shampoos have pH levels of 7-10 (alkaline). The scalp's natural pH is 4.5-5.5. Alkaline shampoos disrupt the acid mantle, favor pathogenic microbes, open the hair cuticle (causing frizz and color fade), and can trigger compensatory sebum overproduction.

Key ingredient in 9:1 Shampoo: No dimethicone (a coating silicone that can trap buildup). Gentle surfactants that cleanse without stripping the microbiome. pH-balanced to 4.5-5.5.

Transition period: When switching from sulfate to sulfate-free, expect 2-4 weeks of adjustment as the scalp recalibrates sebum production. Less lather does not mean less cleaning.

Step 2: Repair

Purpose: Restore moisture and nourishment so the scalp can regulate itself again.

How: Use products with active ingredients that support barrier health and reduce inflammation. Use a scalp mask or balancing serum 1-2x/week. Prioritize consistency over product cycling.

Deep Reference - Repair

Hyaluronic acid: Can hold up to 1,000x its weight in water. Low molecular weight HA penetrates deeper into skin layers; high molecular weight HA forms a protective film on the surface. Research confirms significant improvement in skin hydration and barrier function with topical application.

Green tea extract (EGCG): Antimicrobial against scalp pathogens. May help block DHT (the hormone implicated in pattern hair loss). Reduces inflammatory markers. Helps normalize sebaceous gland activity.

The microbiome connection: A balanced scalp microbiome performs critical functions: pH regulation, inflammation control, barrier defense, nutrient cycling. Dysbiosis (imbalance) can trigger chronic inflammation that pushes follicles prematurely into the resting phase.

Step 3: Reinforce

Purpose: Protect from stressors that damage the scalp barrier and follicles over time. Growth means nothing if the hair breaks before it's visible.

How: Treat your scalp like an extension of your face. Use a leave-in with UV protection. Wear a hat in strong sun. Change your part occasionally to reduce repeated UV exposure in one line. Wear loose styles - avoid tight ponytails.

Deep Reference - Reinforce

UV damage to hair follicles (2019 study): UV radiation reduces hair shaft elongation, causes premature catagen entry (the hair stops growing too early), reduces hair matrix cell proliferation, decreases hair shaft diameter, and triggers perifollicular mast cell degranulation (inflammation). UVA + UVB combination can trigger follicle miniaturization.

Air pollution effects (2020 study): Particulate matter (PM) increases reactive oxygen species (ROS) in follicular keratinocytes. Elevated inflammatory cytokines IL-6 and IL-8 suppress hair shaft elongation. Matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-1, MMP-3) were upregulated more than threefold, indicating structural damage to surrounding tissue.

Hard water: Hard water (212+ ppm CaCO3) causes calcium and magnesium deposition on the hair shaft - 3x more calcium and 4x more magnesium compared to distilled water. This mineral buildup can affect hair texture and potentially clog follicles.

Gherardini J, et al. UV radiation of scalp skin induces hair follicle damage. Int J Cosmet Sci. 2019;41(2):164-182 [VERIFIED]
Jun MS, et al. Particulate matters induce apoptosis in human hair follicular keratinocytes. Ann Dermatol. 2020;32(5):388-394 [VERIFIED]
Srinivasan G, et al. Effects of hard water on hair. Int J Trichology. 2013;5(3):137-9 [VERIFIED]

Step 4: Reactivate

Purpose: Stimulate circulation and release tension that keeps the scalp inflamed.

How: Use a scalp gua sha 3-5x per week (sandalwood preferred). Move the skin with light-medium pressure, don't scratch. Sleep on silk or satin pillowcases. Never sleep on wet hair.

Reset
9:1 Shampoo
Purifying, pH-balanced, no dimethicone, sulfate-free
Repair
9:1 Conditioner
Hyaluronic acid, green tea extract, barrier support
Reinforce
9:1 Leave-In
UV protection, barrier strengthening, daily defense
Reactivate
Sandalwood Gua Sha
Circulation, tension release, meridian pathway work
Keep It Educational, Not Salesy

When presenting the products, frame them as solutions mapped to the system, not as a sales pitch. The products should feel like a natural conclusion to the education. Mention them once, clearly. Don't push. The kits are already in front of each guest.

Jade's Story

This is the emotional heart of the class. Jade shares her own experience. Keep it to 2-3 minutes - honest, personal, not rehearsed-sounding.

Tina's transition line: "Jade, tell them how this all started."
Jade's story beats:
1. "In my early twenties, my scalp turned reactive. Excessive shedding, and a scalp that never felt calm no matter what I tried."
2. "I went to a dermatologist. They prescribed minoxidil and steroids. But my scalp was already so sensitive and unpredictable, I was cautious."
3. "That's when my mom stepped in and said something I'll never forget: 'Calm your scalp first. Then decide.'"
4. "She put me on the same protocol she'd been using with clients for decades. Within weeks, my scalp felt different. Within months, the shedding slowed."
5. "That experience is why AWARE exists. We built the products we wished we'd had."
Why This Story Matters

The stress-scalp connection: Jade's experience illustrates the cortisol cascade. Chronic stress activates the HPA axis, producing persistent cortisol elevation. Hair follicles contain cortisol receptors - chronic exposure can push follicles from growth phase to resting phase prematurely, cause follicle miniaturization, and impair stem cell function needed for new growth cycles.

The barrier-first approach: Tina's advice ("calm your scalp first") reflects trichological best practice: address the scalp environment before introducing active treatments. A compromised barrier won't respond well to stimulating ingredients - it needs to be stabilized first. This is the philosophy behind the 4R order.

Why AWARE was created: Tina saw the same gap for 40 years: "haircare" makes strands look great but ignores the scalp. "Scalp care" treats symptoms but isn't built for salon-treated hair or long-term barrier support. AWARE bridges that gap.

5

Daily Routine & Q&A

10:45 - 10:55 (10 min) - Both

The 5-Minute Daily Ritual

Recap the practical takeaway. This maps to the handout card every guest has in front of them.

Transition line: "Flip your card over - this is your at-home cheat sheet. Everything we covered today in a 5-minute daily ritual."

The daily routine:

  • Wash days (2-3x/week): Double cleanse with the shampoo (Reset), condition mid-lengths to ends (Repair), apply leave-in to scalp and lengths (Reinforce)
  • Daily (3-5x/week): Gua sha for 3-5 minutes using the 4-step sequence (Reactivate). Best before washing or as part of your evening routine.
Key phrase: "The most important thing isn't perfection - it's consistency. Three to five minutes, three to five times a week. That's it. Your scalp responds to routine."

Scalp Environment Report

Introduce the free personalized report. This is where environmental factors become personal.

Key phrase: "One more thing I want to show you. Once we stabilized Jade's scalp, one thing became obvious - it wasn't just what she used, it was what she was exposed to every day. Hard water, pollution, UV."
CTA: "Scan the QR code on your handout card. It takes you to a free personalized report that shows the environmental factors affecting your scalp based on your location - UV levels, water hardness, air quality. It adjusts the 4R recommendations for your specific environment."
Deep Reference - Environmental Factors

6 environmental scalp stress factors:

  • UV Index: Damages follicular keratinocytes, triggers premature catagen, reduces hair shaft diameter
  • Air Quality (AQI): PM2.5 and PM10 cause oxidative stress, inflammatory cytokine release, keratinocyte apoptosis
  • Water Hardness: Calcium/magnesium mineral deposits build up on scalp and hair, affecting texture and potentially blocking follicles
  • Humidity: Extremes in either direction stress the scalp barrier and affect sebum regulation
  • Temperature: Cold reduces circulation; heat increases sebum production and sweating
  • Ozone: Depletes natural antioxidants in hair fiber, causes oxidative damage

Each factor scored 0-100 based on location data. The report personalizes the 4R protocol based on the guest's local conditions.

Q&A Reference

Common questions and prepared answers. Reference name tent concerns that haven't been addressed yet.

"How often should I use the gua sha?"
3-5 times per week, 3-5 minutes per session. Best before washing or as part of your evening routine. You don't need to do it every day - consistency over intensity.
"Can I use it on a sensitive scalp?"
Yes, just use lighter pressure. The sandalwood is naturally smooth and gentle. If your scalp is currently irritated or inflamed, start very light and build up gradually. If it's severely irritated, consult a professional first.
"What's the difference between this and a scalp massager?"
Gua sha follows meridian pathways and works with the fascia. The broad, flat surface moves the scalp tissue differently than bristles or nodules. It's based on thousands of years of traditional practice, and the specific movements are designed to release tension along fascial lines.
"Do I need to use AWARE products with the gua sha?"
The gua sha works on its own. But combining it with the 4R system addresses the complete scalp environment - cleansing, nourishing, protecting, and activating. Each step supports the others.
"How do I clean the gua sha?"
Wipe with a soft cloth after use. Store in the pouch, away from moisture. Sandalwood is naturally antimicrobial, so it stays cleaner than stone or metal tools.
"Does this cure hair loss?"
Gua sha and good scalp care support the environment where hair grows. Hair loss can have many causes - hormones, genetics, stress, nutrition, medications. If you're experiencing significant changes, we always recommend seeing a dermatologist alongside maintaining good scalp health. What we can do is give your follicles the best possible conditions.
"Why is it so expensive?" (if asked about the kit)
These are professional-grade, salon-exclusive formulations. The same quality your stylist uses and recommends. The travel kit lets you experience the full system at a lower entry point.
"Can I buy this on Amazon?"
AWARE is only available through salons and our website. We made that choice so you always have a professional you can ask questions. It's part of the salon-exclusive model.
"I already use [competitor brand]"
That's great that you're already thinking about your scalp. Everyone's scalp environment is different, and the most important thing is having a consistent routine. If what you're using is working for you, that's wonderful.
Medical/diagnosis questions
"That's really a question for a dermatologist. What I can tell you is what we see in the salon and how to maintain your scalp health day-to-day. If you're experiencing sudden patchy loss, pain, burning, or scarring, please see a dermatologist as soon as possible."
6

Warm Close

10:55 - 11:00 (5 min) - Both

Closing Notes

Thank you: "Thank you for spending your morning with us. I hope you learned something about your scalp that you didn't know before."
Consultation offer (mention once): "If you'd like a deeper dive, the code on your card gets you 15% off a one-on-one consultation. No pressure, it's there if you want it."
Stay and mingle: "We're going to stay for a bit, so if you have individual questions, if you want to finish your scalp scan with Jade, or if you just want to practice the gua sha technique one more time, we're here."
Social: "If you take any photos today, tag us @awarehair. We'd love to see your gua sha practice."
Tone Check

The close should feel warm and grateful, not transactional. The consultation offer is mentioned once, briefly, as an option. Don't repeat it. Don't add urgency. The education is the value - the products and consultation are there for people who want to go deeper.

•

  • Jimenez F, et al. Distribution of human hair in follicular units. Dermatol Surg. 1999;25(4):294-8. [VERIFIED]
  • Phillips CM, Weston K. Anatomy, Head and Neck, Scalp. StatPearls. 2023. [VERIFIED]

Environmental Factors

  • Gherardini J, et al. Transepidermal UV radiation of scalp skin ex vivo induces hair follicle damage. Int J Cosmet Sci. 2019;41(2):164-182. [VERIFIED]
  • Kim MJ, et al. Modeling of solar UV-induced photodamage on the hair follicles in human skin organoids. J Tissue Eng. 2024;15. [VERIFIED]
  • Jun MS, et al. Particulate matters induce apoptosis in human hair follicular keratinocytes. Ann Dermatol. 2020;32(5):388-394. [VERIFIED]
  • Srinivasan G, et al. Effects of hard water on hair. Int J Trichology. 2013;5(3):137-9. [VERIFIED]
  • Trüeb RM, et al. Scalp condition impacts hair growth and retention via oxidative stress. Int J Trichology. 2018;10(6):262-270. [VERIFIED]

Ingredient Science

  • Zouboulis CC, et al. Sebaceous immunobiology - skin homeostasis, pathophysiology, coordination of innate immunity. Front Immunol. 2022;13:1029818. [VERIFIED]
  • Vary JC Jr. Selected Disorders of Skin Appendages. Med Clin North Am. 2015;99(6):1195-211. [VERIFIED]
Citation Integrity Note

All citations marked [VERIFIED] were confirmed through PubMed, PMC, or original journal databases. No scalp-specific gua sha studies exist in peer-reviewed literature - the gua sha evidence is from body tissue studies with mechanisms applicable to scalp. When teaching, frame gua sha benefits as "supported by research on how gua sha works on tissue" rather than "proven to grow hair."
